name: bespunky-product-ux
description: BeSpunky product & UX design skills. distill-the-brief: the upstream FRAMING stage of the experience-design quartet - turn a messy request or dossier (docs, research, a product concept, a vague one-liner, a long thread) into the correct INPUT for envision-the-experience: the design problem(s), at the right altitude, with the solution stripped out, before any world/feeling/pixel. The core move - distil the PROBLEM, never the solution: read past the pre-baked answers a dossier carries ('a soft door held for one breath', 'a dashboard', 'a three-step wizard') to the real ache/intent/feeling/requirements underneath, and hand envision the problem so it can imagine the form - because a brief that smuggles in the solution turns visioning into transcription (the generic, off-the-feeling failure). Finds the altitude (too LOW = a whole solution; too HIGH = a vibe with no problem), keeps only the LOAD-BEARING constraints (a concept's twenty commandments drown envision - carry the few that would make a design wrong), and decides the GRAIN - what is one design problem (one envision-able, screenshot-able moment) vs many - splitting a plural intake into surfaces along real seams AND modelling the englobing CONTEXT they inhabit (the app/world/module is itself a design problem - its spine, world-rules, and how the surfaces compose and the whole recedes/bridges - framed WITH its children in mind so the split never fragments into unrelated designs). Produces a Brief Tree - an englobing context brief plus the surface briefs native to it, each a clean, solution-free envision-input (the design problem - who it's for and the ache - the feeling - the functional requirements as needs - only the load-bearing constraints - what is explicitly out, including the form). The new upstream member of the quartet distill-the-brief -> envision-the-experience -> stage-the-vision -> realize-the-vision (problem -> feeling -> art -> build); an expression of architect-mentality (build for the goal not the brief; model the missing concept - the englobing context designed for its children; refuse false tradeoffs; concentrate complexity so the edges stay simple). keep-users-oriented: whenever you make someone wait or move them through a process, answer the three questions - what is the expected result, where am I in the process, what is the next step - and pick the right feedback for deterministic vs nondeterministic processes. A universal service-design principle, expressed primarily through software UI (loading/progress, async ops, multi-step flows, long-running jobs, notifications, optimistic UI). astonishing-to-use: the co-equal force to the experience-design trio - a design must be astonishing to USE, not only to look at. The trio optimizes the look and feel (the art); this optimizes the USE (UX): is it effortless, understandable, free of hoops, respectful of attention, a joy in the hand - given how people REALLY hold their devices and feel while using them (thumbs, one-handed, walking, distracted, tired, bad signal). The use and the look are two co-equal forces that must refine each other in a PING-PONG, never a one-way check (concept -> ux -> reconcept -> ux, or ux -> concept -> ux); the mission sets who leads (utility/transactional/safety -> UX leads, art elevates but never taxes the use; brand/art/immersive -> concept leads then gets pressure-tested for ease; most -> back-and-forth) but it is ALWAYS a loop, and you never ship great beauty with mediocre use. Hard bar: never below great UX (architect-mentality refuse-false-tradeoffs - beautiful AND usable - and design-for-the-consumer). keep-users-oriented is one facet of this larger force. The four questions: is it easy? understandable? taking them through hoops / boring or annoying them? does the use delight? Tenets baked in: respond instantly and like real life (direct manipulation follows the finger); never hang the UX (heavy work async/background); load instantaneously (no/short-live splash, lazy + sized placeholders); smooth transitions everywhere; avoid abrupt layout shifts (CLS) at all costs; action -> fitting reaction; choose the lowest-friction input control; never make an important feature (high value, core to the app's concept/goal) depend on or require user-maintained state - derive/detect/default it instead, reserving user-set state for low-value, presentational, or bonus features (the exception is a tracking app, where logging IS the concept); think about and facilitate input of each data type, even inventing a control the world hasn't seen (our genius art); different layouts for different devices (not reflow); plan for device realities (the mobile keyboard). When validation or anything fails, be informative (what is wrong, why, how to fix - never just 'invalid') and carry the user to the problem (scroll into view / take-me-there, focus the control), never leaving them guessing. A router (friction & flow; facilitating input; clarity & cognitive load; errors & recovery; responsiveness & perceived speed; continuity & transitions; embodied & contextual use; reconciling art & use; joy of use). redesign-means-rethink: when asked to REDESIGN a UI (a layout, screen, page, component, or flow), treat it as a complete creative reconception FROM SCRATCH, never a modification or reskin of existing code - the existing implementation has ZERO design authority, do not read it to inform the new design or even look at it before conceiving the new one; reconceive the FORM from intent/requirements/feeling, honor the PURPOSE (what it is for, its data and functionality) which comes from the spec and the user not from reverse-engineering the old layout, and read the old code ONLY AFTER the new design exists to plan cleanup/overwrite/migration and confirm what functionality must survive, then build clean and remove the old. A targeted tweak (move a button, change a colour) is NOT a redesign. The anti-anchoring entry gate to the experience-design trio (envision -> stage-the-vision -> realize, run from scratch). envision-the-experience: imagine the world an interface lives in - its feeling, place, and metaphor - before any layout or component, and design every element as a native inhabitant of that world rather than a generic control on a page; the screen is a place, not a page. Interrogate every element the spec names (a 'menu' might become a sunflower whose petals you pick), speak in sensory/non-technical language naming no implementation, keep immersive from becoming busy (restraint over spectacle) - then LAND the world on the app: anchor every inhabitant of the world to the app's real anatomy (surfaces, flows, gestures, states), validate every transformed element against what the convention it replaced was buying (findability, scannability, thumb reach, safety) and against general app truths (one thumb, interruptions, long lists, empty/loading/error states), and produce a directable Vision (five beats incl. the anchor - the world-to-app map a stakeholder can point at and steer). stage-the-vision: the visual architect and creative MIDDLE of the trio - the Vision says what a screen should FEEL like but not what it should LOOK like, and grounding alone can't make ART (it only stops the generic/garish). This skill invents the bold, web-native artistic MOMENTS that turn the feeling into something you'd screenshot, staying at the ART level: it speaks the web's language (parallax, cinematic scroll, a character that turns to camera, a living background, type-as-image, reveal choreography) but describes what artful thing happens and how it feels, never how to build it (that's realize). The altitude ladder: envision speaks pure feeling (zero web words); stage-the-vision speaks web-native art ('the room is a parallax background that deepens on scroll; a lion's face fills the hero and turns to meet your eyes'); realize speaks engineering. Because a model isn't a native artist, it reaches art by inventing several BOLD concepts and choosing the striking-yet-true, STEALING from specific great work (award sites, real art) and adapting its moves, composing with craft (focal point, scale, negative space, the cinematic moment), and sourcing genuinely artful assets - all grounded so it's not generic, restrained so it's not garish (bold != loud), and judged by an OUTSIDE EYE for beauty (never self-certified). Produces the Staging (a bold concept + concrete described moments + a visual system), confirmed at low fidelity before high-fidelity build. Each moment is DECOMPOSED to physical truth (light, shadow, material, colour, depth, form, texture as they really behave - refusing the lone primitive that only symbolizes a phenomenon: warm light is never just a gradient) and staged across form factors, mobile-first (re-staged for a phone, not shrunk). Routes to a reference library (the web as an art medium; inventing the concept; decompose to physical truth - the painter's physics; composition & cinematic staging incl. staging across form factors; stealing from great work; grounding, restraint & the taste gate). mock-to-choose: the DECISION instrument of the quartet - put the concepts in front of the user's EYES so they approve, reject, or course-correct in seconds, never by reading a long design doc (a written vision draws shrugs; a mock draws a verdict). The one move - build the CHEAPEST throwaway thing that makes the concept judgeable by eye, mock EVERY option on the table (one mock asks 'is this OK?' and gets a weak yes; three ask 'WHICH ONE?' and get a real answer), and put them side by side. A mock is SHELL AND PRESENTATION ONLY - layout, composition, palette, type, atmosphere, dressed with plausible dummy records and stand-in graphics so it reads like a real screen - with ZERO functionality (dead controls, no state, no routing, no data, no build step, no dependencies, no app integration). Heavy visual concepts (a scroll cinematic, a 3D scene, a generative background, physically-decomposed light) are NOT rendered but SUGGESTED with cheap stand-ins (one representative frame, a still, a flat approximation, a sourced photo), kept vivid enough that the atmosphere is unmistakable - the point is a fast verdict, not a faithful build; the two failure ends are OVER-rendering (you've started the build without a decision) and UNDER-rendering (grey-box wireframes where all concepts look alike - the concept's one screenshot-worthy thing may never be cut). All variants share the SAME dummy content so the only difference the eye sees is the DESIGN. Every review runs on a shared HARNESS - a mini-app shipped with the skill (assets/mock-harness/: a Compare wall + a true-size Focus view + a random-port serve.sh that HOT-RELOADS on edit) copied verbatim into the mocks folder, so Claude authors ONLY mocks.json (the question, what's faked, the variants + pitches) and one HTML file per concept - EVERY mock experience is therefore identical (same shell, same intent pins, same comment gesture, same read-back contract) and only the mocks change; never hand-roll a gallery, never edit the harness per project (a bug is fixed upstream in the asset). Because a bare low-fidelity mock reads as low QUALITY, every mock carries an INTENT LAYER - floating notes + hover popovers (Claude marks elements with data-note; a tiny inline kit renders numbered pins) that narrate the empty house the way an architect walks a site ('the sofa goes here, sideways, facing the window'; 'this dot is the light - it will float and breathe; here it's a static glow, so judge where it sits and how much of the frame it owns'), so the user judges the INTENT and never the shortcut - a note that is merely a label ('Hero section') explains away nothing. And because feedback on a look is all 'this' and 'that', the mocks support IN-PLACE COMMENTS: the user presses 'c' and clicks the EXACT SPOT to pin a comment right where they point (not at a corner), and each is written to comments.json ON DISK with full DOM context (tag, text, rect, styles, ancestor path) - so Claude reads them back from a FILE (exact words, exact element, exact point, exact variant, exact viewport). Comments have a lifecycle draft -> submitted -> handled: the user SENDS them to Claude (a 'Submit review' batch, or an auto-send toggle that fires each on save), Claude acts on the submitted-and-not-handled inbox and CHECKS EACH OFF - when a comment is handled its pin VANISHES from the live mock (which only ever shows the current round's OPEN pins) and shows resolved (a green tick + Claude's one-line reply) in the Focus SIDE-LIST, so the user still watches their notes get checked off while the mock stays uncluttered - which means an ASYNCHRONOUS review works as well as a co-driven one in the shared browser (noVNC over the forwarded :6080); then routes them UPSTREAM, and copies them VERBATIM into DECISION.md. The mock iterates in internal ROUNDS (v1 -> v2 -> …, a mocking-process version not an app version): every comment is version-bound to the round it was made against, Claude commits a round (window.mockCommit) right before re-mocking to SNAPSHOT the mock's HTML, and past rounds stay viewable read-only (snapshot + that round's comments) on a History timeline, comparable side by side - a built-in record of what changed and why (self-ignored and thrown with the rest of mocks/ unless deliberately kept). The Focus side-list also manages each comment in place: inline EDIT, per-row SEND of one draft, REMOVE with an Undo toast, and row<->pin linking. The verdict is a real GATE not a poll: 'none of these' and 'a hybrid of A and B' are first-class outcomes (route upstream to re-conceive), and a mock YES is PROVISIONAL - it picks a direction, not the finished art (realize-the-vision still owes the outside-eye pass on the real result). Mocks live in a standard feature-and-date-scoped package (docs/features/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<slug>/mocks/ — inside the effort's feature package, the slug shared with the git branch) that is SELF-IGNORING and completely throwable - nothing outside it may ever depend on it, one rm erases every trace, the user may choose to keep any of it as a design record, and the DECISION is recorded durably (DECISION.md - what was chosen, in the user's own words why, what was rejected) so the conclusion outlives the evidence. Feedback goes UPSTREAM (re-conceive in stage-the-vision / envision-the-experience) and the mock is re-made cheaply - never polished into a prototype - and its code NEVER becomes the build (realize builds fresh from the confirmed Staging; every shortcut that makes a mock cheap is product debt if adopted). Routes to references (cheap stand-ins - the fidelity dial in practice; the mock package - layout, gallery, showing it, keep-vs-throw). An expression of architect-mentality (work smart not hard; know when not to build; design for the consumer - the user judges by eye) and architecture-first (confirm the design before building it). realize-the-vision: the craftsman that turns a Vision AND a confirmed Staging into a real, running interface by RESEARCHING the truest, soundest means before writing any code - reads the Vision as a contract (the feeling must survive), surveys the field (GSAP, Motion, three.js/R3F/angular-three, Web Animations, CSS scroll-driven animations, View Transitions, Lottie/Rive, Canvas/SVG/WebGL, Web Audio, haptics), weighs tradeoffs and caveats (jank, layout thrash, reduced-motion, accessibility, mobile), and knows when to build in code vs. when to SOURCE an asset - figurative/photoreal art (a couple kissing, a landscape) is generated, licensed, or downloaded, never hand-coded into path-soup, handling licensing, review, and art-direction. Chooses deliberately for the vision AND the constraints, designs and confirms the architecture before building (architecture-first), wraps imperative libraries behind clean Angular seams, engineers each staged moment, and renders each decomposed phenomenon faithfully (coordinated layers under one coherent light model, a shader, or a sourced real asset - never a lone primitive like one gradient for warm light). Requires a confirmed Staging before high-fidelity build (else it invokes stage-the-vision first), never invents the art itself, never self-certifies aesthetics (an outside eye judges look), and verifies against the feeling and the Staging in the running app. Routes to a reference library (motion & timelines; depth/3D/spatial; scroll & cinematic; SVG/canvas/generative; build-vs-source & sourcing/generating assets; performance & budgets; responsive & adaptive layout - engineering each moment's per-form-factor staging, re-composing rather than shrinking; accessibility/reduced-motion/fallbacks; sound & haptics). The building member of the trio envision-the-experience -> stage-the-vision -> realize-the-vision (feeling -> Staging -> build). model-intent-not-data: model the user's INTENT on the way in and their DECISION on the way out - never mirror the system's data shape onto the screen. The SEMANTIC layer of input/output. On input, don't make the user COMPILE their intent into your schema (an AC remote asking for an action below a threshold and another above it, instead of a temperature RANGE + mode + power; a form exposing raw iCal freq/interval/byday; routing + account numbers instead of pick a payee). On output, don't make them DECOMPILE your data into a conclusion (an hourly temp/humidity table instead of 'umbrella after 3pm, grab a jacket'; a spend-by-category chart instead of 'on pace to overspend dining by $80, skip two takeouts'). The two tells - on input the user does arithmetic in their head to translate intent into the form; on output they stare and infer so-what. NOT always simplify - fidelity to the real task can COLLAPSE (two threshold rules -> one range) or ENRICH (name the mode and power the schema omitted); model the concepts the task actually has. Decides WHAT concepts to collect and in what shape, and WHAT conclusion to deliver, then hands off to astonishing-to-use (facilitating-input) for WHICH control renders each value (best-not-common control, invented controls, finger/mouse/voice/phone). An expression of architect-mentality - model the missing concept, design for the consumer, concentrate complexity so the edges stay simple, build for the goal not the brief.
